claim: The center of the granuloma in an immune cell induces an anti-inflammatory immune response.

result.claim: The center of the granuloma in an immune cell induces an anti-inflammatory immune response.
result.reasoning: The claim that "The center of the granuloma in an immune cell induces an anti-inflammatory immune response" is refuted by evidence from paper 3475317, "Inflammatory signaling in human Tuberculosis granulomas is spatially organized." This paper explicitly states that "The centers of granulomas have a pro-inflammatory environment" (Proposition ID: prop_1177). This pro-inflammatory environment is further characterized by the presence of antimicrobial peptides (prop_1178), reactive oxygen species (prop_1179), and pro-inflammatory eicosanoids (prop_1180). In contrast, the paper clarifies that "the tissue surrounding the caseum has an anti-inflammatory signature" (Proposition ID: prop_1181), indicating that anti-inflammatory responses are located in the periphery, not the center, of the granuloma. The abstract of paper 3475317 directly supports these findings, stating, "We found that the centers of granulomas have a pro-inflammatory environment... Conversely, the tissue surrounding the caseum has a comparatively anti-inflammatory signature."

evidence.text: The centers of granulomas have pro-inflammatory eicosanoids present.
evidence.source: Inflammatory signaling in human Tuberculosis granulomas is spatially organized

evidence.text: The centers of granulomas have a pro-inflammatory environment.
evidence.source: Inflammatory signaling in human Tuberculosis granulomas is spatially organized
